coredata.dc:title: Hyaluronate dots for highly efficient photodynamic therapy
coredata.dc:creator: Choi, Eun Jung
Lee, Jae Min
Youn, Yu Seok
Na, Kun
Lee, Eun Seong
coredata.dc:description: Nanoscale particles, such as quantum dots and carbon dots, are important materials for use in sensing and treating irregular biological events, but their versatility for biomedical applications are usually limited by their undesirable properties such toxicity and non-degradability. Here, we report biofunctional hyaluronic acid (HA) dots containing biodegradable/biocompatible HA. HA dots were prepared by conjugating multiple HA molecules to C60 (used as a base dot) without any hydrothermal treatment. The hydroxyl groups of HA completely linked to all π-π carbon bonds in C60. The chemically synthesized HA dots (Mn=16.1kDa) were 2nm in diameter, soluble in aqueous solution, and possessed multiple functional (carboxyl) groups. The HA dots were biofunctional, enabling highly efficient binding to CD44 receptors overexpressed on in vitro/in vivo tumors. With light illumination, we demonstrated that the HA dots bearing a photosensitizing model drug (chlorin e6: Ce6) resulted in a significant enhancement in in vitro/in vivo tumor cell ablation. We believe that this approach offers a new strategy to create biopolymer dots.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hyaluronic acid dots
Biopolymer dots
Photodynamic tumor therapy

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst pubtype ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: In vivo therapeutic potential of dHA-Ce6

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Science, ICT and Future Planning

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korean government (MSIP) (No. NRF-2015R1A4A1042350 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Synthesis scheme of HA dots with Ce6 (dHA-Ce6). (b) Schematic concept of dHA-Ce6. See the text for more details.

(a) TEM image of dHA-Ce6 and cHA-Ce6. (b) Zeta potential values of dHA-Ce6, dHA, cHA-Ce6, and cHA (0.1mg/mL, pH 7.4, 150mM) (n=3). (c) UV–vis spectra of each sample and free Ce6 in PBS (pH 7.4, 150mM). (d) The emission spectra (at λex 400nm and λem 600–750nm) of each sample (10μg/mL) in DMSO solvent.

The 9,10-dimethylanthracene (DMA) fluorescence change (at λex 360nm and λem 380–550nm) of dHA-Ce6 (equivalent Ce6 10μg/mL), cHA-Ce6 (equivalent Ce6 10μg/mL), HA-Ce6 (equivalent Ce6 10μg/mL), free Ce6 (10μg/mL), dHA (100μg/mL), cHA (100μg/mL), and HA (100μg/mL) in PBS (150mM, pH 7.4). The singlet oxygen generation is indicated by the DMA fluorescence intensity change (Ff–Fs, where Ff is the fluorescence intensity of pure DMA and Fs is the fluorescence intensity of DMA mixed with the sample). All samples were illuminated for 10min at a light intensity of 5.2mW/cm2 using a 670nm laser source.

Phototoxicities were determined by a CCK-8 assay of (a) HCT-116 or (b) KB cells treated with each sample (equivalent Ce6 0.1–10μg/mL) or free Ce6 (0.1–10μg/mL) for 4h. All of the cells were illuminated for 10min at a light intensity of 5.2mW/cm2 using a 670nm laser source and were incubated for an additional 12h (n=7) (** p<0.01 compared to free Ce6). (c) Flow cytometry analysis of HCT-116 cells treated with each sample (equivalent Ce6 10μg/mL) or free Ce6 (10μg/mL) for 4h.

Confocal images of (a) HCT-116 and (b) KB cells treated with each sample (equivalent Ce6 10μg/mL) or free Ce6 (10μg/mL) at 37°C for 4h. The treated cells were stained using DAPI and WGA-Alexa Fluor® 488.

(a) In vivo noninvasive photoluminescent tumor imaging of each sample (equivalent Ce6 2.5mg/kg) or free Ce6 (2.5mg/kg) injected intravenously into HCT-116 tumor-bearing nude mice. Fluorescent tumor images were obtained at 1, 4, 8, and 24h post-injection. (b) Uptake of intravenously injected samples (equivalent Ce6 2.5mg/kg) or free Ce6 (2.5mg/kg) in each organ that was extracted 72h post-injection. The tumor site is indicated by a red dashed circle. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

The relative tumor volume change (Vt/V0, where Vt is the tumor volume at a given time, and V0 is the initial tumor volume) of HCT-116 tumor-bearing nude mice. The mice were locally irradiated (at a light intensity of 5.2mW/cm2 for 40min by using a 670nm laser source) at 12h post-injection of each sample (equivalent Ce6 2.5mg/kg), free Ce6 (2.5mg/kg) or control (PBS) (n=5) (** p<0.01 compared to control).

(a) Fluorescence images of the sliced tumors and (b) histological analysis (by hematoxylin and eosin staining) of the tumor sections. (c) The relative body weight change (Wt/W0, where Wt is the body weight at a given time, and W0 is the initial body weight) of HCT-116 tumor-bearing nude mice locally irradiated (n=5) (** p<0.01 compared to control).

head.ce:title: Hyaluronate dots for highly efficient photodynamic therapy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biotechnology, The Catholic University of Korea, 43 Jibong-ro, Bucheon-si, Gyeonggi-do 14662, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, SungKyunKwan University, 2066 Seobu-ro, Jangan-gu, Gyeonggi-do 16419, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nanoscale particles, such as quantum dots and carbon dots, are important materials for use in sensing and treating irregular biological events, but their versatility for biomedical applications are usually limited by their undesirable properties such toxicity and non-degradability. Here, we report biofunctional hyaluronic acid (HA) dots containing biodegradable/biocompatible HA. HA dots were prepared by conjugating multiple HA molecules to C60 (used as a base dot) without any hydrothermal treatment. The hydroxyl groups of HA completely linked to all π-π carbon bonds in C60. The chemically synthesized HA dots (Mn=16.1kDa) were 2nm in diameter, soluble in aqueous solution, and possessed multiple functional (carboxyl) groups. The HA dots were biofunctional, enabling highly efficient binding to CD44 receptors overexpressed on in vitro/in vivo tumors. With light illumination, we demonstrated that the HA dots bearing a photosensitizing model drug (chlorin e6: Ce6) resulted in a significant enhancement in in vitro/in vivo tumor cell ablation. We believe that this approach offers a new strategy to create biopolymer dots.

ce:section.ce:para: Quantum dots have great promise in biomedical fields because of their strong photoluminescence, tunable fluorescence emission, and surface functionalization (Duarte, 2016; Huang & Lovell, 2017; Saha & Viswanatha, 2017; Vamivakas et al., 2010; Youn, Kwag, & Lee, 2017). The newly emerged carbon dots, which are produced by hydrothermal treatment, are an alternative to the classic metal-based quantum dots and are highly photostable, photoluminescent, and possess good optoelectronic properties (Lim, Shen, & Gao, 2015; Zhang et al., 2017). These dots promise to be attractive nanomaterials in that particles with a few nanometers in size are believed to results in improved performance in cells expressing various biological signals or genetic changes (Chan, Xu, Kwon, Kietrys, & Kool, 2017; Kumar, Bhushan, & Gopinath, 2017; Ran et al., 2017; Xiao, Wang, Huang, Wong, & Sun, 2017; Zhang et al., 2017). Indeed, numerous studies have shown that carbon dots show improved permeability in impaired tissues, efficiently enter cells or cellular organelles, affect distinctive biological functions, and ensure high signal for detecting diseases (Choi et al., 2007). It is important to note that these unique and favorable features of dots have been utilized to probe and explain a variety of biological events, but their undesirable properties, especially their non-degradability and non-biocompatibility (toxicity) in the body, are still debated (Choi et al., 2007; Kim & Oh, 2017; Park, Yu, & Jung, Mok, 2017; Zhang et al., 2017). The development of simple, safe, and versatile platforms for generating organic dots (alternative to traditional dots) is therefore a challenging issue. The fabrication of organic dots with diverse biofunctionalities is expected to gain much attention with their extensive applications to biomedical, cosmetic fields, and biological science fields (Choi et al., 2007; Kwag, Oh, & Lee, 2014; Lee et al., 2012; Lee, Youn, Park, & Lee, 2014; Lim et al., 2015; Oh, Kwag, Oh, Youn, & Lee, 2012; Saha & Viswanatha, 2017; Vamivakas et al., 2010; Zhang et al., 2017).

Motivated by this rationale, we tried to synthesize hyaluronic acid (HA) dots. HA is a linear biodegradable polysaccharide, found primarily in the extracellular/pericellular matrix, especially in connective tissues such as the synovial joints (Kim, Oh, Youn, & Lee, 2014). It is also known that high concentrations of HA can promote malignant tumor proliferation, and antagonists to HA/CD44 receptor binding events can inhibit tumor growth (Kim et al., 2014; Kwag et al., 2014; Toole, 2009). Given this fact, the HA/CD44 receptor can be applicable as a biomarker for malignant tumors (Kim et al., 2014; Toole, 2009). Here, we utilized a chemical conjugation method, as opposed to the conventional hydrothermal carbonization method (Lim et al., 2015; Zhang et al., 2017) used for carbon dot fabrication, to achieve biofunctional HA dots. We used C60 (∼1nm in diameter) as a base dot. HA was completely linked to all π-π carbon bonds in C60 (Fig. 1 a), producing biodegradable/biocompatible HA dots (hereafter denoted as dHA). Importantly, these conjugates with a few nanometers in size can be completely cleared from the body, reducing safety concerns in clinical applications (Choi et al., 2007; Duarte, 2016; Lim et al., 2015). It is remarkable to note that the novel approaches in polysaccharide conjugate design have brought about significant advances in pharmaceutics or cosmetics (Park et al., 2011; Liu et al., 2015; Liu et al., 2017; Yang et al., 2012). Fabricating biopolymer dots preserving their original biofunctionality is challenging. We believe that this approach offers a new strategy to create biopolymer dots.

Hyaluronic acid (HA, Mn =3.7kDa), lithium hydroxide (LiOH), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), N,N′-dicyclohexylcarbodiimide (DCC), adipic acid dihydrazide (ADH), triethylamine (TEA), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), toluene, 9,10-dimethylanthracene (DMA), formaldehyde, and 4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole dihydrochloride (DAPI) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (USA). Chlorin e6 (Ce6) was obtained from Frontier Scientific Inc. (USA). C60 was purchased from NanoLab Inc. (USA). RPMI-1640, fetal bovine serum (FBS), penicillin, trypsin, ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA), and streptomycin were purchased from Welgene Inc. (Korea). Wheat Germ Agglutinin Alexa Fluor® 488 conjugate (WGA-Alexa Fluor® 488) was purchased from Life Technologies (USA). Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) was obtained from Dojindo Molecular Technologies Inc. (Japan).

The hydroxyl groups of HA (1.5g) were conjugated to all π-π carbon bonds of C60 (160mg) using LiOH (100mg) as a catalyst in DMSO (20mL)/toluene (20mL) co-solvent at room temperature for 3days (Fig. 1a). After the reaction, toluene in the solution was evaporated using a rotary evaporator. The resulting solution was dialyzed using a pre-swollen dialysis membrane tube (Spectra/Por® MWCO 10kDa) in fresh DMSO for 2days, then in deionized water for 2days to remove non-reacted chemicals and lyophilized (Kim et al., 2014; Kwag et al., 2014; Lee et al., 2012; , 2014; Oh et al., 2012; Park et al., 2011). Next, the obtained HA dots (dHA, 400mg) were preactivated using DCC (20mg) and NHS (25mg) in DMSO (20mL) for 1day and then reacted with Ce6 [250mg, preactivated (reaction time: 4h) using DCC (20mg), NHS (25mg), ADH (35mg), and TEA (0.5mL) in DMSO (20mL)] at room temperature for 2days, producing dHA with Ce6 (dHA-Ce6) (Fig. 1a). The reacted solution was dialyzed using a pre-swollen dialysis membrane tube (Spectra/Por® MWCO 10kDa) in fresh DMSO for 2days, then in deionized water for 2days and lyophilized (Kim et al., 2014; Kwag et al., 2014; Lee et al., 2012; , 2014; Oh et al., 2012). Additionally, HA clusters (cHA, as a control group) were prepared through the chemical crosslinking of dHA (200mg) using ADH (14mg), DCC (50mg), NHS (70mg), and TEA (0.5mL) in DMSO (20mL) at room temperature for 2days. After the reaction, the solution was dialyzed using a pre-swollen dialysis membrane tube (Spectra/Por® MWCO 20kDa) and lyophilized (Kim et al., 2014; Kwag et al., 2014; Lee et al., 2012; , 2014; Oh et al., 2012). The obtained cHA (40mg) were preactivated using DCC (2mg) and NHS (2.5mg) in DMSO (2mL) for 1day and then reacted with the preactivated Ce6 (25mg) for 2days, producing cHA with Ce6 (cHA-Ce6). As another control group, an HA/Ce6 conjugate (HA-Ce6) was prepared after the simple conjugation reaction (reaction time: 2days) between HA (400mg) and the preactivated Ce6 (250mg) in DMSO (40mL) containing DCC (20mg) and NHS (25mg).
The chemical structure of each sample was analyzed using a Bruker 300MHz NMR Spectrometer (Bruker, Germany) (Kim et al., 2014; Kwag et al., 2014; Lee et al., 2012; , 2014; Oh et al., 2012).

The number average molecular weight (Mn) of dHA was determined using a an HPLC system (Waters, USA) equipped with a 410 differential refractometer and a gel permeation chromatography (GPC) KF-804 or GPC KF-805 column (Shodex, Japan). The flow rate was 1.0mL/min and DMF was used as the mobile phase at room temperature (Kim et al., 2014; Kwag et al., 2014; Lee et al., 2012; , 2014; Oh et al., 2012).

The particle size and surface morphology of each sample was confirmed using a transmission electron microscope (TEM; JEM 1010, Japan) (Kim et al., 2014; Kwag et al., 2014; Lee et al., 2012; , 2014; Oh et al., 2012).

The zeta-potential of each sample (0.1mg/mL) in PBS (pH 7.4, 150mM) was measured with a Zetasizer 3000 (Malvern Instruments, USA) (Kim et al., 2014; Kwag et al., 2014; Lee et al., 2012; , 2014; Oh et al., 2012). The UV–vis spectra of dHA-Ce6 (equivalent Ce6 10μg/mL), cHA-Ce6 (equivalent Ce6 10μg/mL), HA-Ce6 (equivalent Ce6 10μg/mL), free Ce6 (10μg/mL), dHA (100μg/mL), cHA (100μg/mL), and HA (100μg/mL) in PBS (pH 7.4, 150mM) were monitored at 300–800nm. The light emission spectrum (at λex 400nm and λem 600–750nm) of each sample (10μg/mL) in DMSO solvent was monitored using a Shimadzu RF-5301PC spectrofluorometer (Japan) (Kim et al., 2014; Kwag et al., 2014; Lee et al., 2012; , 2014; Oh et al., 2012). The generation of singlet oxygen by dHA-Ce6 (equivalent Ce6 10μg/mL), cHA-Ce6 (equivalent Ce6 10μg/mL), HA-Ce6 (equivalent Ce6 10μg/mL), free Ce6 (10μg/mL), dHA (100μg/mL), cHA (100μg/mL), and HA (100μg/mL) in PBS (pH 7.4, 150mM) was confirmed using fluorescent 9,10-dimethylanthracene (DMA). Briefly, DMA (20mmol) was mixed with each sample in PBS. The solution was illuminated at a light intensity of 5.2mW/cm2 using a 670nm laser source for 10min. When the DMA fluorescence intensity (measured using a Shimadzu RF-5301PC spectrofluorometer at λex 360nm and λem 380–550nm) reached a plateau after 1h, the change in DMA fluorescence intensity was plotted. Here, singlet oxygen generation is indicated by the DMA fluorescence intensity change (Ff–Fs, where Ff is the fluorescence intensity of pure DMA and Fs is the fluorescence intensity of DMA mixed with the sample) (Kim et al., 2014; Kwag et al., 2014; Lee et al., 2012; , 2014; Oh et al., 2012).

Human colon carcinoma HCT-116 cells and human epidermoid carcinoma KB cells (from the Korean Cell Line Bank) were maintained in RPMI-1640 medium with 1% penicillin-streptomycin and 10% FBS in a humidified standard incubator with a 5% CO2 atmosphere at 37°C. Prior to testing, the cells (106/mL) were grown as a monolayer and harvested by trypsinization using a 0.25% (wt./vol.) trypsin and 0.03% (wt./vol.) EDTA solution and seeded in wells of plates containing the RPMI-1640 medium (Kim et al., 2014; Kwag et al., 2014; Lee et al., 2012; , 2014; Oh et al., 2012). The phototoxicity of each sample with light illumination was tested for HCT-116 or KB tumor cells. The cells were incubated with each sample (equivalent Ce6 0.1–10μg/mL) or free Ce6 (0.1–10μg/mL) at 37°C for 4h and then washed three times with PBS (pH 7.4). Subsequently, the cells were illuminated with a light intensity of 5.2mW/cm2 using a 670nm laser source for 10min and further incubated at 37°C for 12h. Cell viability was determined using a Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8 assay). Additionally, without illumination, the cytotoxicity of each sample was evaluated after 24h of treatment (Kim et al., 2014; Kwag et al., 2014; Lee et al., 2012; , 2014; Oh et al., 2012).

HCT-116 or KB cells were incubated with each sample (equivalent Ce6 10μg/mL) or free Ce6 (10μg/mL) for 4h at 37°C and then washed three times with PBS (pH 7.4). Subsequently, the cellular uptake of each sample or free Ce6 was monitored using a FACSCalibur™ Flow Cytometer (Becton Dickinson, USA). Next, to verify the distribution of each sample in cells, HCT-116 or KB cells were incubated with each sample (equivalent Ce6 10μg/mL) or free Ce6 (10μg/mL) and then stained using DAPI and WGA-Alexa Fluor® 488 to visualize the cell nuclei and cell membranes. The treated cells were examined using a confocal laser-scanning microscope (CarlZeiss Meta LSM510, Germany) (Kim et al., 2014; Kwag et al., 2014; Lee et al., 2012; , 2014; Oh et al., 2012).

In vivo studies were conducted with 7-week old female nude mice (BALB/c, nu/nu mice, Institute of Medical Science, Japan). Mice were maintained under the guidelines of an approved protocol from the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee (IACUC) of the Catholic University of Korea (Republic of Korea) (Kim et al., 2014; Kwag et al., 2014; Lee et al., 2012; , 2014; Oh et al., 2012).

To prepare in vivo xenografted tumors, HCT-116 tumor cells (1×107 cells in PBS pH 7.4) were subcutaneously injected into female nude mice. When the tumor volume reached 100mm3, each sample (equivalent Ce6 2.5mg/kg), free Ce6 (2.5mg/kg) or control (PBS) was intravenously injected into the tumor-bearing nude mice through their tail vein. Fluorescence images were obtained at 1, 4, 8, and 24h post-injection using an Image Station 4000 MM (Kodak, USA) with a special C-mount lens and a long-wave emission filter (Omega Optical, USA). In particular, the organ tissues (brain, lung, heart, liver, spleen, kidney, and tumor) extracted from nude mice (72h post-injection) euthanized via carbon dioxide asphyxiation were analyzed using an Image Station 4000 MM. Furthermore, the excised tumor tissues were fixed in a 10wt.% formaldehyde solution, embedded in a paraffin block, and then sectioned at 4μm thickness on a microtome. The resulting specimens were further analyzed using an Image Station 4000 MM. The tumor specimens stained with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) were visualized using a slide scanner (Aperio CS2, Leica Biosystems, Inc., Germany) (Kim et al., 2014; Kwag et al., 2014; Lee et al., 2012; , 2014; Oh et al., 2012).

To evaluate the therapeutic potential of dHA-Ce6, each sample (equivalent Ce6 2.5mg/kg), free Ce6 (2.5mg/kg) or control (PBS) were intravenously injected into HCT-116 tumor-bearing nude mice through the tail vein. At 12h post-injection, the tumor sites of the nude mice were locally illuminated for 40min at a light intensity of 5.2mW/cm2 with a 670nm laser source. Tumor volume was calculated using the formula: tumor volume=length×(width)2/2. The relative tumor volume change (V/V0), where V is the tumor volume at a given time and V0 is the initial tumor volume, was plotted to evaluate the photodynamic tumor ablating ability of dHA-Ce6. The relative body weight change (Wt/W0, where Wt is the body weight at a given time, and W0 is the initial body weight) of HCT-116 tumor-bearing nude mice was plotted to estimate the potential toxicity of each sample for the whole body (Kim et al., 2014; Kwag et al., 2014; Lee et al., 2012; , 2014; Oh et al., 2012).

All of the results were analyzed via the Student’s t-test or ANOVA at a significance level of p<0.01 (**). The MINITAB® release 14 statistical software program was used for all statistical analyses (Kim et al., 2014; Kwag et al., 2014; Lee et al., 2012; , 2014; Oh et al., 2012).

As shown in Fig. 1a, the hydroxyl groups of HA completely linked to all π-π carbon bonds in C60 using lithium hydroxide (LiOH) as a catalyst. 13C NMR analysis demonstrated that the π-π carbon bonds in C60 were completely substituted to form carbon single bonds (Fig. S1). From 1H NMR peaks, the generation of protons (resulting HA coupling) at the carbon single bonds of C60 were detected at δ 1.23 (Fig. S1). The degree of HA coupling (defined as the number of HA molecules per C60 molecule) was estimated from the 1H NMR peaks using the integration ratio of the peaks from δ 1.23 (CH, C60) and δ 2.43 (CH2
, HA). As a result, the average HA coupling was 4.2, indicating that the molecular weight of HA dots (dHA) is 16.3kDa (Mn). A gel permeation chromatography (GPC) analysis also confirmed that the molecular weight of dHA is 16.1kDa (Mn). The resultant dHA were then coupled to an anticancer model drug, photosensitizing chlorin e6 (Ce6) (Oh et al., 2012), using dicyclohexylcarbodiimide (DCC) and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) (Fig. 1a). According to the results of 1H NMR, an average of 4.3mol of Ce6 were coupled with 1mol of dHA, estimated using the integration ratio of the peaks from δ 1.23 (CH, C60) and δ 2.80 (CH 2 , Ce6) (Fig. S1). This dot has a hollow core (due to the oringial C60 strucuture) and HA shells expressing Ce6 (Figs. 1 a and 2 a ), and is expected to efficiently bind to CD44 receptor-rich tumor cells (Kim et al., 2014; Kwag et al., 2014; Toole, 2009), resulting in Ce6-mediated photodynamic tumor ablation (Fig. 1b). As a result, biofunctional dHA were successfully synthesized, without the conventional hydrothermal carbonization process of HA at high temperature (Lim et al., 2015; Zhang et al., 2017). Additionally, we prepared various combinations of control groups to evaluate the potential of dHA. First, an HA cluster (cHA) was prepared through the chemical crosslinking of dHA using difunctional adipic acid dihydrazide (ADH) as a crosslinker (Fig. 2a). The resultant cHA was then coupled to Ce6, producing cHA with Ce6 (cHA-Ce6, Fig. 2a). 1H NMR analysis confirmed that an average 4.5mol of Ce6 were coupled with 1mol of dHA in cHA (data not shown). Linear HA was also coupled to Ce6, producing HA with Ce6 (HA-Ce6). Similarly, from the 1H NMR analysis, an average of 1.2mol of Ce6 were found to couple with 1mol of HA (data not shown).

Fig. 2a shows the dHA-Ce6 images obtained using a transmission electron microscope (TEM). The TEM image reveals that dHA-Ce6, which was on average 2nm in diameter is dot in shape. In contrast, cHA-Ce6 (as a control group) was on average 40nm in diameter and spherical in shape. Alternatively, HA-Ce6 (as another control group) is water-soluble and has no detectable particle size. Fig. 2b shows that HA particles (dHA-Ce6, dHA, cHA-Ce6, and cHA) have negative zeta potentials (ranging from −13mV to −30mV), owing to the presence of the negatively charged HA shell and Ce6. Not surprisingly, the coupling of Ce6 to HA particles further decreased their zeta potential values. The UV–vis spectra of Ce6 conjugates (dHA-Ce6, cHA-Ce6, and HA-Ce6) showed distinct light absorption bands at 400, 500, and 650–670nm, consistent with typical light absorption bands for free Ce6 (Fig. 2c) (Oh et al., 2012). The structural variation of HA particles (dHA-Ce6, cHA-Ce6, and HA-Ce6) seemed to slightly influence UV–vis absorption bands of Ce6. In the emission spectra (at λex 400nm) of water-soluble Ce6 conjugates (dHA-Ce6, cHA-Ce6, and HA-Ce6), we observed increased near infrared (NIR) fluorescence intensities at 670nm. This phenomenon may be due to the dequenched state of Ce6 that is scattered in an HA backbone, unlike free Ce6 molecules that are involved in auto-quenching behaviours (Oh et al., 2012; Park et al., 2011) (Fig. 2d).

The generation of singlet oxygen from Ce6 conjugates, determining phototoxicity, was confirmed using fluorescent 9,10-dimethylanthracene (DMA) (Oh et al., 2012; Park et al., 2011). DMA is extremely fast at trapping singlet oxygen, which causes a reduction of its fluorescence (Oh et al., 2012; Park et al., 2011). As shown in Fig. 3 , the DMA fluorescence intensity change (Ff-Fs) indicates the generation of singlet oxygen under light illumination (Oh et al., 2012; Park et al., 2011). The experimental results demonstrated that water-soluble Ce6 conjugates (dHA-Ce6, cHA-Ce6, and HA-Ce6) highly generates singlet oxygen as a result of the dequenching event of Ce6 molecules. However, free Ce6 produced a reduced amount of singlet oxygen as a result of the auto-quenching event (Oh et al., 2012) of Ce6 self-aggregation in PBS. These results are partially corelated with the Ce6-mediated phototoxicity in tumor cells.

Fig. 4 a and b shows the in vitro phototoxicity of Ce6 conjugates in tumor cells (HCT-116 and KB). While being illuminated, free Ce6 was less phototoxic to tumor cells as compared to dHA-Ce6. The enhanced phototoxicity of dHA-Ce6 in HCT-116 cells indicated their high singlet oxygen generation and extensive uptake in HCT-116 cells expressing CD44 receptors (Kim et al., 2014; Kwag et al., 2014; Toole, 2009). Remarkably, the phototoxicity of dHA-Ce6 in HCT-116 cells was significantly greater than that of cHA-Ce6 and HA-Ce6 at a concentration equivalent to Ce6 at 5–10μg/mL (Fig. 4a). It is noteworthy that the difference in phototoxicity of dHA-Ce6, cHA-Ce6, and HA-Ce6 in KB cells (CD44 receptor-negative cells) (Kwag et al., 2014; Park et al., 2011; Ue et al., 1998) was not significant (Fig. 4b). Overall, these results suggest that the dot structure of dHA strongly influences the equilibrium dynamics of the ligand-receptor biding pocket, although it is unclear how dHA affects receptor dynamics (Serohijos et al., 2011). Fig. 4c also supports this finding, with the Ce6 fluorescence of HCT-116 cells treated using dHA-Ce6 being approximately 66-, 4.8-, and 3.3-fold higher than that in cells treated with free Ce6, HA-Ce6, and cHA-Ce6, respectively.

In confocal image studies of HCT-116 or KB cells (stained with DAPI and WGA-Alexa Fluor® 488 to visualize the cell nuclei and membranes) (Lee et al., 2014) treated with dHA-Ce6, the detection of Ce6 fluorescence enables tracking of dHA-Ce6 in cells (Oh et al., 2012). Fig. 5 a and b confirmed that the highest cellular uptake in HCT-116 tumor cells was achieved with dHA-Ce6 treatment. The Ce6 fluorescence of dHA-Ce6 was primarily observed in the cytoplasm (or perinuclear region) and cell membrane, reflecting their efficient CD44 receptor binding ability on HCT-116 cells. In additon, as shown in Fig. 5b, the limited cellular uptake of dHA-Ce6 in KB cells (CD44 receptor-negative cells) are responsible for the reducued phototoxicity of dHA-Ce6 (Fig. 4b).

We further investigated the tumor uptake of dHA-Ce6 in vivo. For this purpose, we prepared an HCT-116 tumor xenograft model and intravenously injected Ce6 conjugates into tumor-bearing nude mice. Fig. 6 a and b shows typical serial NIR photoluminescence images (Choi et al., 2017) of the nude mice treated with dHA-Ce6. Significantly, dHA-Ce6 resulted in high-resolution photoluminescence at the tumor site (24–72h post-injection) and the reduced Ce6 photoluminescence in normal organs (brain, lung, heart, liver, spleen, and kidney), owing to their prolonged Ce6 retention at the tumor site and efficient clearance in nontumoral organs. In contrast, cHA-Ce6, which was relatively highly populated in tumor site, still displayed high photoluminescence in the liver and lung at 72h post-injection. Despite heavy blood flow and fenestrated blood vessels, our dHA-Ce6 seemed to be efficiently extravasated into tumors. It was also interesting to note that the dots with sizes below 5.5nm have rapid and efficient renal clearance (Choi et al., 2007).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo therapeutic potential of dHA-Ce6
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 7 shows the in vivo therapeutic efficiency of dHA-Ce6 in the tumor-bearing nude mice. Under local light illumination, dHA-Ce6 dramatically decreased tumor volume. The relative tumor volume in nude mice given dHA-Ce6 was approximately 2.3-, 3.9-, and 10-fold smaller than that in mice treated with cHA-Ce6, free Ce6, and control (PBS), respectively (Fig. 7). To evaluate these results, we also sliced the tumor tissues treated with each sample and analyzed them. Fig. 8 a shows that dHA-Ce6 was efficiently delivered deep into the tumor tissues, as visualized in the tumor slices. These dHA-Ce6 seemed to heavily induce tumor cell death during photodynamic therapy (Fig. 8b), suggesting the successful tumor treating ability of dHA. Although extensive toxicological evaluations have not been performed yet, no significant changes in the body weight among nude mice treated with each sample were observed (Fig. 8c).

In this study, we successfully fabricated biopolymer dots, and demonstrated that dHA is biofunctional and represents a more effective modality in tumor targeting and therapy. This system possesses great potential as an advanced technology platform based on dot structure. We believe that our method can further satisfy the need for building organic dots for various applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korean government (MSIP) (No. NRF-2015R1A4A1042350).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbpol.2017.10.047

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
